name: dojo
context: Dojo registry ecosystem — spec, PR-based contribution flow, knowledge endpoints, and runtime architecture
info: Top-level map of the Dojo skill registry and knowledge graph. Dojo indexes agent-facing capabilities as a tree of manifests, exposes rich knowledge endpoints for search, learning, backlinks, and graph traversal, and accepts contributions through a standard GitHub pull-request workflow. Start here to navigate the spec, contributor guide, or architecture reference.
triggers: how do i contribute to dojo
how does dojo load skill trees
what is a good dojo node
how does the knowledge graph work
body:
# Dojo

Dojo is both a skill registry and a knowledge graph for agent-facing capabilities. Every capability — from deploying an AWS Lambda to transferring an ERC-20 token — lives as a `skill.json` manifest in the `examples/` tree.

## Quick navigation

- [[dojo/spec]] — manifest schema, hierarchy rules, and API contract
- [[dojo/contributing]] — fork, branch, PR contributor workflow
- [[dojo/architecture]] — runtime loading, search indexing, and knowledge endpoint model

## How it works

The `examples/` tree is the runtime source of truth. The registry server walks it on boot, indexes every manifest, and serves the result through a REST API. The web app reads from that API — it never touches the filesystem directly.

Rich fields like `body`, `sections`, `aliases`, `links`, and `related` are not cosmetic: they drive the web knowledge view, backlinks, and agent learning flows. A manifest with only `context` and `tags` will resolve and execute, but it won't teach agents anything through `/v1/learn` or appear in backlink graphs.

## Design principles

1. **Manifests are the API.** A well-written `skill.json` is discoverable by agents, readable by humans, and indexable by search — no separate docs layer needed.
2. **Knowledge is first-class.** Nodes carry long-form prose (`body`, `sections`), semantic links (`links`, `related`), and alternate names (`aliases`) that power `/v1/learn`, `/v1/backlinks`, and `/v1/graph`.
3. **Contributions go through GitHub PRs.** Every change — new ecosystems, new skills, edits — lands via pull request against the `examples/` tree. There is no separate publish command.
4. **The tree is the product.** Ecosystems like [[ethereum]], [[aws]], [[docker]], and [[github]] are peers. Dojo itself is just another ecosystem that documents how the registry works.

## Example: a minimal vs rich manifest

A **minimal** manifest passes validation but doesn't teach:
```json
{"name": "my-skill", "version": "1.0.0", "uri": "my-eco/my-skill",
 "type": "skill", "context": "Does something", "info": "More detail",
 "tags": ["example"], "parent": "my-eco"}
```

A **rich** manifest teaches agents and humans:
```json
{"name": "my-skill", "version": "1.0.0", "uri": "my-eco/my-skill",
 "type": "skill", "context": "Does something specific and useful",
 "info": "Detailed explanation of when and why to use this...",
 "tags": ["example", "tutorial"], "parent": "my-eco",
 "aliases": ["the thing that does X"],
 "triggers": ["how do i do X", "set up X for me"],
 "body": "# My Skill\nLong-form explanation...",
 "sections": [{"id": "usage", "title": "Usage", "body": "...", "tags": ["howto"]}],
 "links": [{"uri": "my-eco/related-skill", "context": "Depends on this"}],
 "related": [{"uri": "other-eco/similar", "relation": "alternative"}]}
```

The second manifest appears in search, teaches through `/v1/learn`, generates backlinks, and helps agents route to it via natural-language triggers.

sections.title: Start here
sections.body: If the question is about manifest shape or parent rules, go to [[dojo/spec]]. If the question is about opening a PR or validating locally, go to [[dojo/contributing]]. If the question is about why something shows up in the UI or search, go to [[dojo/architecture]].

sections.title: What makes a good Dojo node
sections.body: A strong node has: (1) a short `context` (max 200 chars) that lets an agent decide in one glance whether this node is relevant, (2) a fuller `info` that explains when and why to use it, (3) natural-language `aliases` and `triggers` so agents and humans can find it by how they think about it — not just by its URI, (4) long-form knowledge in `body` plus `sections` that teaches the concept, (5) `links` for directed navigation to prerequisites and related nodes, and (6) `related` for softer semantic ties like alternatives or evolutions. Executable nodes also need `scripts` with `schema` for input/output contracts. Compare the [[ethereum/erc20]] manifest (rich) with a bare minimum manifest to see the difference in practice.

sections.title: Recommended layout vs allowed layout
sections.body: For human contributors, the easiest default is ecosystem -> standard -> skill -> sub. Example: `ethereum` (ecosystem) -> `ethereum/erc20` (standard) -> `ethereum/erc20/transfer` (skill) -> `ethereum/erc20/transfer/batch` (sub). The spec is more flexible: `standard`, `skill`, and `context` nodes may live under any non-`sub` parent, while `sub` still requires a `skill` parent. See [[dojo/spec#hierarchy]] for the full rule set.

sections.title: Knowledge API surface
sections.body:
The registry exposes two layers of API:

**Core routes** handle execution: `/v1/resolve` (manifest lookup), `/v1/skills/*` (CRUD), `/v1/search` (full-text), `/v1/tree/*` (subtree traversal), `/v1/agent/ask` (agent query endpoint).

**Knowledge routes** handle learning: `/v1/ecosystems` (list roots), `/v1/learn/*` (rendered body + sections), `/v1/backlinks/*` (who links here), `/v1/graph/*` (local neighborhood), `/v1/alias/:alias` (resolve alternate name), `/v1/agent/learn` (agent knowledge query).

Search quality depends on index coverage. If `aliases`, `body`, and `sections` aren't indexed, richer manifests won't improve search results. See [[dojo/architecture#search-index]] for details.

sections.title: Contribution model
sections.body: All changes to the skill tree land through GitHub pull requests. The workflow: fork `keychain-inc/dojo`, create a feature branch, add or edit manifests under `examples/`, validate with `dojo validate`, smoke-test against the local registry server, and open a PR. Maintainers review for schema validity, content quality, and search coverage. Squash-merge lands the node into the live registry. See [[dojo/contributing]] for the complete step-by-step guide.

sections.title: Ecosystem peers
sections.body: Dojo is one ecosystem among many. Current peers include [[ethereum]] (smart contracts, tokens, DeFi), [[aws]] (Lambda, S3, IAM), [[docker]] (containers, images, compose), [[github]] (repos, auth), [[stripe]] (payments, subscriptions), [[postgres]] (queries, migrations), [[redis]] (cache, pubsub), [[openai]] (chat, embeddings), [[slack]] (messages, channels), [[notion]] (pages, databases), [[vercel]] (deployments, domains), and [[twilio]] (SMS, voice). Each ecosystem follows the same manifest schema and can cross-link via `links` and `related`.

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.body:
**My node doesn't appear in search.** Check that `tags`, `aliases`, and `body` are populated — thin manifests rank low. Restart the server to re-index. See [[dojo/architecture#search-index]].

**My node fails validation.** Run `dojo validate ./skill.json` and check for: `context` over 200 chars, invalid `type`/`parent` combo, missing required fields, or bad URI pattern. See [[dojo/spec#manifest-core]].

**My backlinks are missing.** Backlinks are computed from `links` and `related` fields. If node A links to node B, B's backlinks page shows A. Check that your `links[].uri` values are correct and that the target node exists.

**My ecosystem doesn't show in the web UI.** The web app reads from `/v1/ecosystems`. Make sure your root `skill.json` has `type: ecosystem` and `parent: null`, and that the server has restarted. See [[dojo/architecture#ecosystems-endpoint]].
